# Passage:
Saccharomyces cerevisiae is a single-celled eukaryotic organism that makes Protein X. The rate of incorporation of radiolabeled amino acids into Protein X was measured in the experiment described below.  
At time = 0 s, radiolabeled amino acids were added to a flask containing S. cerevisiae in a growth medium. After 50 s, a large excess of nonradioactive amino acids was added.  
Aliquots of cells were removed at 20 s intervals after the addition of the radiolabeled amino acids. The cells were separated from the growth medium, placed in a buffer solution, and blended thoroughly. An antibody to Protein X was added, which caused the fully translated Protein X to precipitate. The radioactivity of the precipitate was determined and plotted as a function of time (Figure 1).  
this graph shows a linear increase in radioacitvity from 0-300 seconds (plateaus at 4 cpm).
Graph stayss at 4 cpm until 1200 seconds then declines exponentially until roughly zero at 1800 seconds.  
Figure 1Protein X synthesis in S. cerevisiae
Two hypotheses were proposed to explain the sudden decline in radioactivity that occurred after about 1200 s.  
Hypothesis 1  
The decline is the result of random, nonspecific degradation of the protein by proteolytic enzymes.  
Hypothesis 2  
The decline occurs when a precursor to Protein X is modified to produce a mature form of Protein X that is subsequently secreted by the cell.
